# Figure description: скв_142.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/raw_graphics/Колонки_ скважин/скв_142.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

This is a **well log / borehole log (каротажная диаграмма)** — a composite geophysical well log typical of Soviet-era exploration drilling. It appears to be a multi-track wireline log displayed on a long vertical strip of paper (характерный формат советских каротажных диаграмм), likely produced by a field logging crew. The format is consistent with standard Soviet геофизический каротаж (geophysical well logging) practices of the 1970s–1980s.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log covers a single borehole (скважина), likely one of the exploration/parametric wells drilled as part of the regional survey described in Report 36572. The vertical axis represents **depth**, appearing to span from near-surface down to approximately **1,200–1,500 metres** based on the length and scale divisions visible. The log is divided into multiple vertical tracks across its width, characteristic of a composite log showing several geophysical parameters simultaneously.

The header area (top of the log) contains borehole identification information, survey parameters, and instrument/scale data, though the resolution makes specific borehole numbers difficult to confirm with certainty. The log may correspond to one of the parametric or structural boreholes drilled in the M-42 or N-42 map sheet areas of northern Kazakhstan.

---

## Key Content

The composite log appears to contain the following tracks (left to right):

**Left margin columns:**
- Lithological/stratigraphic column with formation descriptions, likely including rock type symbols (штриховка) indicating sandstones, siltstones, clays, and possibly crystalline basement rocks at depth
- Depth scale markings at regular intervals (likely every 10 or 20 metres)
- Stratigraphic horizon labels and formation boundaries marked with horizontal lines

**Central tracks (primary geophysical curves):**
- **КС (кажущееся сопротивление / apparent resistivity)** curves — the most prominent oscillating curves running through the central portion of the log. These show significant lateral deflections indicating contrasting rock resistivities. High-resistivity excursions (deflections to the right) likely correspond to competent rocks (carbonates, crystalline basement, cemented sandstones), while low-resistivity zones indicate clays, saturated sediments, or weathered material
- **ПС (потенциал самополяризации / spontaneous polarization / SP)** curve — a smoother curve adjacent to the resistivity track, used for lithological correlation and identification of permeable beds
- Possibly **ГК (гамма-каротаж / gamma-ray log)** — a curve sensitive to clay content and radioactive minerals

**Right margin columns:**
- Lithological description column with written rock descriptions
- Porosity, permeability, or core analysis data
- Stratigraphic/formation age assignments
- Possibly notes on mineralization, water-bearing horizons, or casing depths

**Notable features visible:**
- A prominent high-amplitude anomaly zone visible in the upper-middle portion of the log (approximately 200–400 m depth range), where the resistivity curves deflect strongly — this may indicate a significant lithological boundary, mineralized horizon, or hard rock unit
- The lower portion of the log shows more subdued, regular oscillations, possibly indicating more homogeneous sedimentary section
- A distinct boundary approximately two-thirds of the way down the log (possibly a major stratigraphic unconformity or casing shoe depth)
- The very bottom of the log shows renewed curve activity suggesting a return to more variable lithology, possibly pre-Mesozoic basement or a different stratigraphic package

---

## Text and Labels

Due to the low resolution and faded nature of the document (typical of microfilmed or photocopied Soviet technical reports), full transcription is limited. The following elements are identifiable:

**Header (top of log):**
- Title block likely reads: **КАРОТАЖНАЯ ДИАГРАММА** (Karotazhnaya diagramma — Well Log)
- Borehole number (скв. №): partially legible, possibly a 3-digit number
- Location/area reference, possibly referencing the map sheet (лист карты)
- Date of logging
- Logging company/organization name
- Масштаб глубин (depth scale): likely **1:500** or **1:200** (standard Soviet scales)
- Масштаб кривых (curve scale): КС in Ом·м (ohm-metres), ПС in мВ (millivolts)

**Annotations within the log:**
- Depth markers at regular intervals along the left edge (e.g., 100, 200, 300... metres)
- Formation/horizon names written horizontally in Cyrillic alongside the stratigraphic column
- The notation **"Башмак обсадной колонны"** (casing shoe) may be indicated at a specific depth
- Possibly **"Забой"** (total depth/bottom of hole) at the base

**Footer:**
- A small stamp or seal box visible at the lower right, likely containing approval/certification information standard for Soviet technical documents

---

## Research Value

This well log provides critical subsurface lithological and petrophysical data for correlating stratigraphic units penetrated by the borehole, allowing geologists to identify rock types, formation boundaries, and potentially mineralized or fluid-bearing horizons at depth. The resistivity and SP curves together enable identification of permeable beds, clay-rich intervals, and competent basement rocks, which is essential for evaluating the structural framework and mineral potential of the survey area. In the context of Report 36572's regional geophysical survey of northern Kazakhstan, this log serves as ground-truth calibration data for the seismic, gravity, and magnetic surveys, helping to tie surface geophysical anomalies to specific subsurface geology and depth-to-basement estimates.
